output.diagnosis_summary: Acute lameness in a 2‑year‑old male Border Collie with an osteolytic femoral lesion, mild neutrophilia, and inflammatory but culture‑negative joint fluid most likely represents primary bone neoplasia (osteosarcoma). Secondary possibilities include septic arthritis/osteomyelitis or occult fracture.

differential_diagnoses.name: Occult fracture with secondary inflammation
key_features: radiographic lytic area could be periosteal reaction

recommended_additional_tests: CT or MRI of the femur to assess cortical breach and soft‑tissue involvement.
Fine‑needle aspirate (FNA) of the bone lesion for cytology, Gram stain, aerobic/anaerobic culture, and PCR for common pathogens.
Serum calcium, phosphorus, ALP, vitamin D to rule out metabolic bone disease.
Repeat joint fluid culture with extended incubation and anaerobic media.

supportive_care: joint lavage/arthrocentesis to reduce inflammation and obtain fluid for repeat culture.
weight‑bearing restriction with sling or brace; avoid prolonged activity.

osteosarcoma_confirmed: adjuvant carboplatin 200 mg/m² IV q3–4 weeks for 2 cycles if no metastasis
multimodal analgesia (gabapentin, tramadol) for pain management
infection_confirmed: targeted antibiotics based on culture sensitivity for 2–4 weeks
monitor CBC, joint swelling, and pain scores
fracture_confirmed: surgical stabilization (locking plate or intramedullary pin) if displaced; otherwise conservative with cast/bracing

prognosis_and_owner_communication.osteosarcoma: Guarded to poor without amputation; post‑amputation quality of life improved but lifespan ~3–6 months. Discuss euthanasia if pain uncontrolled.
prognosis_and_owner_communication.septic_arthritis: Good prognosis with prompt, appropriate antibiotics; risk of joint destruction and systemic spread.
prognosis_and_owner_communication.fracture: Variable depending on fracture type; potential for full recovery.
